Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## CONTRASTIVE EXPLANATIONS FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING VIA EMBEDDED SELF PREDICTIONS
**Zhengxian Lin, Kim-Ho Lam, Alan Fern** Department of EECS Oregon State University {linzhe, lamki, alan.fern}@oregonstate.edu

## ABSTRACT

We investigate a deep reinforcement learning (RL) architecture that supports explaining why a learned agent prefers one action over another. The key idea is to learn action-values that are directly represented via human-understandable properties of expected futures. This is realized via the embedded self-prediction (ESP) model, which learns said properties in terms of human provided features. Action preferences can then be explained by contrasting the future properties predicted for each action. To address cases where there are a large number of features, we develop a novel method for computing minimal sufficient explanations from an ESP. Our case studies in three domains, including a complex strategy game, show that ESP models can be effectively learned and support insightful explanations.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Traditional RL agents explain their action preference by revealing action _A_ or _B_ ’s predicted values, which provide little insight into its reasoning. Conversely, a human might explain their preference by contrasting meaningful properties of the predicted futures following each action. In this work, we develop a model allowing RL agents to explain action preferences by contrasting human-understandable future predictions. Our approach learns deep generalized value functions (GVFs) (Sutton et al., 2011) to make the future predictions, which are able to predict the future accumulation of arbitrary features when following a policy. Thus, given human-understandable features, the corresponding GVFs capture meaningful properties of a policy’s future trajectories.

To support sound explanation of action preferences via GVFs, it is important that the agent uses the GVFs to form preferences. To this end, our first contribution is the _embedded self-prediction (ESP) model_ , which: 1) directly “embeds" meaningful GVFs into the agent’s action-value function, and 2) trains those GVFs to be “self-predicting" of the agent’s Q-function maximizing greedy policy. This enables meaningful and sound contrastive explanations in terms of GVFs. However, this circularly defined ESP model, i.e. the policy depends on the GVFs and vice-versa, suggests training may be difficult. Our second contribution is the ESP-DQN learning algorithm, for which we provide theoretical convergence conditions in the table-based setting and demonstrate empirical effectiveness.

Because ESP models combine embedded GVFs non-linearly, comparing the contributions of GVFs to preferences for explanations can be difficult. Our third contribution is a novel application of the integrated gradient (IG) (Sundararajan et al., 2017) for producing explanations that are sound in a well-defined sense. To further support cases with many features, we use the notion of minimal sufficient explanation (Juozapaitis et al., 2019), which can significantly simplify explanations while remaining sound. Our fourth contribution is case studies in two RL benchmarks and a complex real-time strategy game. These demonstrate insights provide by the explanations including both validating and finding flaws in reasons for preferences.

**In Defense of Manually-Designed Features.** It can be controversial to provide deep learning algorithms with engineered meaningful features. The key question is whether the utility of providing such features is worth the cost of their acquisition. We argue that for many applications that can benefit from informative explanations, the utility will outweigh the cost. Without meaningful features, explanations must be expressed as visualizations on top of lower-level perceptual information (e.g.

Further, in many applications, meaningful features already exist and/or the level of effort to acquire them from domain experts and AI engineers is reasonable. It is thus important to develop deep learning methods, such as our ESP model, that can deliver enhanced explainability when such features are available.

## 2 EMBEDDED SELF-PREDICTION MODEL

An MDP is a tuple _⟨S, A, T, R⟩_ , with states _S_ , actions _A_ , transition function _T_ ( _s, a, s[′]_ ), and reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ). A policy _π_ maps states to actions and has Q-function _Q[π]_ ( _s, a_ ) giving the expected infinite-horizon _β_ -discounted reward of following _π_ after taking action _a_ in _s_ . The optimal policy _π[∗]_ and Q-function _Q[∗]_ satisfy _π[∗]_ ( _s_ ) = arg max _a Q[∗]_ ( _s, a_ ). _Q[∗]_ can be computed given the MDP by repeated application of the _Bellman Backup Operator_ , which for any Q-function _Q_ , returns a new Q-function _B_ [ _Q_ ]( _s, a_ ) = _R_ ( _s, a_ ) + _β_[�] _s[′][ T]_[(] _[s, a, s][′]_[) max] _[a][′][ Q]_[(] _[s][′][, a][′]_[)][.]

We focus on RL agents that learn an approximation _Q_[ˆ] of _Q[∗]_ and follow the corresponding greedy ˆ policy _π_ ( _s_ ) = arg max _a Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ). We aim to explain a preference for action _a_ over _b_ in a state _s_ , i.e. explain why _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) _> Q_[ˆ] ( _s, b_ ). Importantly, the explanations should be meaningful to humans and soundly reflect the actual agent preferences. Below, we define the embedded self-prediction model, which will be used for producing such explanations (Section 4) in terms of generalized value functions.

**Generalized Value Functions (GVFs).** GVFs (Sutton et al., 2011) are a generalization of traditional value functions that accumulate arbitrary feature functions rather than reward functions. Specifically, given a policy _π_ , an _n_ -dimensional state-action feature function _F_ ( _s, a_ ) = _⟨f_ 1( _s, a_ ) _, . . . , fn_ ( _s, a_ ) _⟩_ , and a discount factor _γ_ , the corresponding _n_ -dimensional GVF, denoted _Q[π] F_[(] _[s, a]_[)][,][is][the][ex-] pected infinite-horizon _γ_ -discounted accumulation of _F_ when following _π_ after taking _a_ in _s_ . To produce human-understandable explanations, we assume semantically-meaningful features are available, so that the corresponding GVFs describe meaningful properties of the expected future—e.g., expected energy usage, or time spent in a particular spatial region, or future change in altitude.

**ESP Model Definition.** Given policy _π_ and features _F_ , we can contrast actions _a_ and _b_ via the GVF difference ∆ _[π] F_[(] _[s, a, b]_[) =] _[ Q][π] F_[(] _[s, a]_[)] _[ −][Q] F[π]_[(] _[s, b]_[)][, which may highlight meaningful differences in how] the actions impact the future. Such differences, however, cannot necessarily be used to soundly explain an agent preference, since the agent may not explicitly consider those GVFs for action selection. Thus, the ESP model forces agents to directly define action values, and hence preferences, in terms of GVFs of their own policies, which allows for such differences to be used soundly.

As depicted in Figure 1, the ESP model embeds a GVF _Q[π] F_[ˆ][of][the][agent’s][greedy][policy] _[π]_[ˆ][into] the agents Q-function _Q_[ˆ] , via _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) = _C_[ˆ] ( _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ )), where _C_[ˆ] : _R[n] → R_ is a learned combining function from GVF vectors to action values. When the GVF discount factor _γ_ is zero, the ESP model becomes a direct combination of the features, i.e. _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) = _C_[ˆ] ( _F_ ( _s, a_ )), which is the traditional approach to using features for function approximation. By using _γ >_ 0 we can leverage human-

provided features in a potentially more powerful way. Because an ESP agent represents action-values via GVF components, it is possible to produce sound contrastive explanations in terms of GVFs, as described in Section 4.

In general, the ability to learn a quality Q-function and hence policy using the ESP model requires that the GVF features are sufficiently expressive. While, in concept, using a single feature equal to the reward is sufficient for learning the Q-function (i.e. the GVF is the Q-function and the identity combining function could be used), that choice does not support explainability. Thus, it is desirable to use a set of features that meaningfully decompose important aspects of the environment and at the same time have GVFs that are expressive enough to combine into the Q-function. In Section 6, we describe the generic schema used for GVF features in our experimental environments.

## 4 CONTRASTIVE EXPLANATIONS FOR THE ESP MODEL

We focus on contrastive explanation of a preference, _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) _> Q_[ˆ] ( _s, b_ ), that decomposes the preference magnitude _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) _− Q_[ˆ] ( _s, b_ ) in terms of components of the GVF difference vector ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ) = _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ ) _− Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, b_ ). Explanations will be tuples _⟨_ ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _, W_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _⟩_ , where _W_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _∈ R[n]_ is an attribution weight vector corresponding to ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ). The meaningfulness of an explanation is largely determined by the meaningfulness of the GVF features. We say that an explanation is sound if _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) _− Q_[ˆ] ( _s, b_ ) = _W_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _·_ ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ), i.e. it accounts for the preference magnitude. We are interested in explanation methods that only return sound explanations, since these explanations can be viewed as certificates for the agent’s preferences. In particular, the definition implies that _W_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _·_ ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _>_ 0 if and only if _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) _> Q_[ˆ] ( _s, b_ ). In the simple case of a linear combining functionˆ ˆ _C_[ˆ] with weights _w ∈ R[n]_ , the preference magnitude factors as _Q_ ( _s, a_ ) _−Q_ ( _s, b_ ) = _w ·_ ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ). Thus, _⟨_ ∆ _F_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _, w⟩_ is a sound explanation for any preference.

**Non-Linear Combining Functions.** Non-linear combining functions are necessary when it is difficult to provide features that support good policies via linear combining functions. Since the above linear factoring does not directly hold for non-linear _C_[ˆ] , we draw on the _Integrated Gradient (IG)_ (Sundararajan et al., 2017), which was originally developed to score feature importance of a single input relative to a “baseline" input. We adapt IG to our setting by treating the less preferred action as the baseline, which we describe below in the terminology of this paper.

Let _Xsa_ = _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ ) and _Xsb_ = _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, b_ ) be the GVF outputs of the compared actions. Given a differentiable combining function _C_[ˆ] , IG computes an attribution weight _θi_ ( _s, a, b_ ) for component _i_ by integrating the gradient of _C_[ˆ] while interpolating between _Xa_ and _Xb_ . ## 5 RELATED WORK

Prior work considered linear reward decomposition models with known weights for speeding up RL (Van Seijen et al., 2017), multi-agent RL (Russell & Zimdars, 2003; Kok & Vlassis, 2004), and explanation (Juozapaitis et al., 2019). This is a special case of the ESP model, with GVF features equal to reward components and a known linear combining function. Generalized value function networks (Schlegel et al., 2018) are a related, but orthogonal, model that combines GVFs (with given policies) by treating GVFs as features accumulated by other GVFs. Rather, our GVFs are used as input to a combining network, which defines the policy used for the GVF definition. Integrating GVF networks and the ESP model is an interesting direction to consider.

The MSX for linear models was originally introduced for MDP planning (Khan et al., 2009) and more recently for reward decomposition (Juozapaitis et al., 2019). We extend to the non-linear case. A recent approach to contrastive explanations (Waa et al., 2018) extracts properties from policy simulations at explanation time (Waa et al., 2018), which can be expensive or impossible. Further, the explanations are not sound, since they are not tied to the agent’s internal preference computation. Saliency explanations have been used in RL to indicate important parts of input images (Greydanus et al., 2018; Iyer et al., 2018; Gupta et al., 2020; Atrey et al., 2020; Olson et al., 2019). These methods lack a clear semantics for the explanations and hence any notion of soundness.

## 6 EXPERIMENTAL CASE STUDIES

Below we introduce our domains and experiments, which address these questions: 1) (Section 6.2) Can we learn ESP models that perform as well as standard models? 2) (Section 6.2) Do the learned ESP models have accurate GVFs? 3) (Section 6.3) Do our explanations provide meaningful insight?

## 6.1 ENVIRONMENT DESCRIPTION

**Schema for Selecting GVF Features.** Before introducing the environments we first describe the schema used to select GVF features across these environments. This schema can serve as a general starting point for applying the ESP model to new environments. In general, episodic environments have two main types of rewards: 1) a _terminal reward_ , which occurs at the end of an episode and can depend on the final state, and 2) _pre-terminal rewards_ , which occur during the episode depending on the states and/or actions. Since the value of a policy will typically depend on both types of rewards, it is important to have GVF features that capture both terminal and pre-terminal that are potentially relevant and interpretable. Thus, in each domain, as describe below, we include simple _terminal GVF features_ that describe basic conditions at the end of the episode (e.g. indicating if the cart went out-of-bound in Cartpole). In addition, we include _pre-terminal GVF features_ that are obtained from the state variables of the environment or derived reward variables that are used to compute the reward function, which are typically readily available from a domain description.

Discrete state or reward variables can simply be encoded as indicator GVF features. For continuous state and reward variables we consider two options: a) When a variable has a small number of meaningful regions, we can use indicator features for the regions as features. The GVFs then indicate how long the agent is in each region; b) We also consider delta GVF features that are equal to the change in a variable across a time step. The GVF value for these features can be interpreted as the future change in the variables’ values. While we focus on the above generic GVF features in this paper, an agent designer can define arbitrary GVF features based on their intuition and knowledge.

**Lunar Lander.** We use the standard OpenAI Gym version of Lunar Lander, a physics simulation game where the agent aims to safely land a rocket ship in a target region by deciding at each step which of three thrusters (if any) to activate. The raw state variables are the position and velocity vectors and the reward function penalizes crashing, rewards landing in the goal area, and includes other "shaping" reward variables. These variables are all easily extracted from the simulation environment. In this domain, the continuous variables do not have intuitively meaningful discretizations, so we use the delta features as the main features for explanation case studies (ESP-continuous). However, to illustrate that learning can be done with the discretization approach in this domain, we also

include results for the continuous features being discretized into 8 uniform bins (ESP-discrete). The pre-terminal features are based on the variables distance-to-goal, velocity, tilt-angle, right landing leg in goal position, left landing leg in goal position, main engine use, side engine use. The terminal feature is an indicator of safely landing.

**Cart Pole.** We use the standard OpenAI Gym Cart Pole environment, a physics simulation where the agent aims to vertically balance a free-swinging pole attached to a cart that can have a force applied to the left or right each step. The state variables include the position, pole angle, and their velocities, and reward is a constant +1 until termination, which occurs when either the pole falls below a certain angle from vertical, moves out of bounds, or after 500 steps.

Since the CartPole variables discretize into a small number of intuitively meaningful regions, we consider both discrete (ESP-discrete) and delta encodings (ESP-continuous) of the GVF features. For ESP-discrete, there are 8 pre-terminal GVF features that discretize the state variables into meaningful regions corresponding to an intuitive notion of safety. This includes two indicators for each variable corresponding to cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity. A perfectly balanced pole will always remain in the defined safe regions. These discretized features also double as the terminal features, since they capture the relevant aspects of the state at termination (being in a safe or unsafe region). For ESP-continuous, we have 12 features, the first 8 pre-terminal GVF features corresponding to the delta features of the cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity for left and right sides. The 4 terminal GVF features are indicators of whether the episode ended by moving out-of-bounds to the left or right or the pole fell below the termination agle to the left or right.

**Tug of War.** Tug of War (ToW) is an adversarial two-player strategy game we designed using PySC2 for Starcraft 2. ToW is interesting for humans and presents many challenges to RL including an enormous state space, thousands of actions, long horizons, and sparse reward (win/loss). A detailed description is in Appendix C. ToW is played on a rectangular map divided into top and bottom horizontal lanes. Each lane has two bases structures at opposite ends, one for each player. The first player to destroy one of the opponent’s bases in either lane wins. The game proceeds in 30 second waves. By the beginning of each wave, players must decide on either the top or bottom lane, and how many of each type of military production building to purchase for that lane. Purchases are constrained by the player’s available currency, which is given at a fixed amount each wave. Each purchased building produces one unit of the specified type at the beginning of each wave. The units move across the lanes toward the opponent, engage enemy units, and attack the enemy base if close enough. The three types of units are Marines, Immortals, and Banelings, which have a rock-paper-scissors relationship and have different costs. If no base is destroyed after 40 waves, the player with the lowest base health loses. In this work, we trained a single agent against a reasonably strong agent produced via pool-based self-play learning (similar to AlphaStar training(Vinyals et al., 2019)).

We present two ToW ESP agents that use 17 and 131 structured GVF features (noting that the 131 features are very sparse). These feature sets are detailed in Appendix E. For the 17 feature agent, the pre-terminal features correpsond to the delta damage to each of the four bases by each of the three types of units; allowing GVFs to predict the amount of base damage done by each type of unit, giving insight into the strategy. Note that there is no natural discretization of the numeric damage variables and hence we only consider the delta encoding. The terminal GVF features are indicators of which base has the lowest health at the end of the game and whether the game reached 40 waves. The terminal GVF features encode the possible ways that the game can end. The 131 feature agent extends these features to to keep track of damage done in each lane to and from each combination of unit types along with additional information about the economy.

## 6.2 LEARNING PERFORMANCE

To evaluate whether using ESP models hurts performance relative to “standard" models we compare against two DQN instances: _DQN-full_ uses the same overall network architecture as ESP-DQN, i.e. the GVF network structure feeding into the combining network. However, unlike ESP-DQN, the DQN-full agent does not have access to GVF features and does not attempt to train the GVF network explicitly. It is possible DQN-full will suffer due to the bottleneck introduced at the interface between the GVF and combiner networks. Thus, we also evaluate _Vanilla DQN_ , which only uses the combining network of ESP-DQN, but directly connects that network to the raw agent input. Details of network architectures, optimizers, and hyperparameters are in the Appendix D.

Figure 2 (top row) shows the learning curves for different agents and for the random policy. All curves are averages of 10 full training runs from scratch using 10 random seeds. For the control problems, CartPole (with discrete and continous GVFs features) and LunarLander, we see that all agents are statistically indistinguishable near the end of learning and reach peak performance after about the same amount of experience. This indicates that the potential complications of training the ESP model did not significantly impact performance in these domains. We see that the discrete feature version of CartPole converged slightly faster than the continous version, but the difference is relatively small. For ToW, the ESP-DQN agents perform as well or better than the DQN variants, with all agents showing more variance. ESP-DQN with 17 features consistently converges to a win rate of nearly 100% and is more stable than the 131-feature version and other DQN variants. Interestingly, DQN-full with 17 features consistently fails to learn, which we hypothesize is due to the extreme 17 feature bottleneck inserted into the architecture. This is supported by seeing that with 131 features DQN-full does learn, though more slowly than ESP-DQN.

To evaluate the GVF accuracy of ESP-DQN we produce ground truth GVF data along the learning curves. Specifically, given the ESP policy _π_ ˆ at any point, we can use Monte-Carlo simulation to estimate _Q[π] F_[ˆ][(] _[s, a]_[)][ for all actions at a test set of states generated by running] _[π]_[ˆ][.][Figure 2 (bottom row)] shows the mean squared GVF prediction error on the test sets as learning progresses. First, for each domain the GVF error is small at the end of learning and tends to rapidly decrease when the policy approaches its peak reward performance. LunarLander and ToW show a continual decrease of GVF error as learning progresses. CartPole, rather shows a sharp initial increase then sharp decrease. This is due to the initially bad policy always failing quickly, which trivializes GVF prediction. As the policy improves the GVFs become more challenging to predict leading to the initial error increase.

## 6.3 EXAMPLE EXPLANATIONS

Appendix F includes a larger set of examples with detailed analysis in each domain.

**Lunar Lander.** In Figure 3a, the game state (top) shows a state in Lunar Lander entered by a near-optimal learned ESP policy. The state is dangerous due to the fast downward and clockwise rotational velocity depicted by arrows. The GVFs (bottom-left) shows the Q-values for the actions and the predicted GVF bars. We see that the “main engine" and “right engine" actions have nearly the same Q-values with "main engine" slightly preferred, while “left engine" and “noop" are considered significantly worse. We want to understand the rationale for the strong and weak preferences.

While a user can observe differences among GVFs across actions, it is not clear how they relate to the reference. The IG and MSX (bottom-right) shows the IGXs corresponding to the preference of “main engine" over the other three actions. In addition, the MSX is depicted via dashed lines over IGX components in the MSX. Focusing first on the larger preferences, “main engine" is preferred to

“left engine" primarily due to GVF differences in the velocity and landing features, with the MSX showing that landing alone is sufficient for the preference. This rationale agrees with common sense, since the left engine will accelerate the already dangerous clockwise rotation requiring more extreme actions that put the future reward related to landing at risk.

For the preference over “noop" the velocity feature dominates the IGX and is the only MSX feature. This agrees with intuition since by doing nothing the dangerous downward velocity will not be addressed, which means the landing velocity will have a more negative impact on reward. Comparing “main engine" to the nearly equally valued “right engine" shows that the slight preference is based on the distance and right leg landing feature. This is more arbitrary, but agrees with intuition since the right engine will both reduce the downward velocity and straighten the ship, but will increase the leftward velocity compared to the main engine. This puts it at greater risk of reducing reward for missing the right leg landing goal and distance reward. Overall the explanations agreed well with intuition, which together with similar confirmation can increase our confidence in the general reasoning of the policy. We also see the MSXs were uniformly very small.

**Cart Pole.** We compare a Cart Pole state-action explanation to an explanation produced by its reversed state as shown in Figure 3b. This comparison illustrates how in one case, the explanation agrees with intuition and builds confidence; while the other exposes an underlying inaccuracy or flaw.

Our original game state (left) positions the cart in a dangerous position moving right, close to the end of the track. The pole is almost vertical and has a small angle velocity towards the left. The action “push left" (move cart left) agrees with intuition as the cart is at the right edge of the screen and cannot move right without failing the scenario. The IG and MSX (left) concurs, showing the cart’s current position close to the right edge as the main reason why it prefers the “push left" action over the “push right"; moving left will put the cart back within a safe boundary.

Reversing the game state (left) by multiplying -1 to each value in the input state vector produces a flipped game state (right). The cart is now positioned in a dangerous position moving left, close to the end of the track. Once again the pole is almost vertical and now has a small angle velocity towards the right. One would expect the agent to perform the action “push right" (the opposite action to game state (left)) as moving left will cause the agent to move off the screen and fail the scenario. However, as depicted in IG and MSX (right) we see the agent prefers “push left" over “push right". The agent justifies this action via an MSX that focuses on maintaining pole vertically to the left. This justification indicates that the agent is putting too much weight on the pole angle versus the boundary condition in this dangerous situation. The agent has not learned the critical importance of the left boundary. This indicates further training on the left side of the game map is needed. Presumably, during training the agent did not experience similar situations very often.

In the second game state (bottom), the ESP agent plays against an opponent that it was not trained against and loses by having the bottom base destroyed. The state shows a large enemy attack in the bottom with the ESP agent having enough resources (1500 minerals) to defend if it takes the right action. However, the most preferred action is to add just one Baneling building to the bottom lane, which results in losing. Why was this mistake made?

We compare the preferred action to an action that adds more buildings to the bottom lane, which should be preferred. The IGX and MSX show that the action preference is dominated by the GVF feature related to inflicting damage in the top lane with Banelings. Thus, the agent is “planning" to save minerals to purchase more top Baneling buildings. The IGX does indicate that the agent understands the sub-optimal action will be able to defend the bottom lane, however, this advantage for the sub-optimal action is overtaken by the optimism about the top lane. This misjudgement of relative values causes the agent to lose the game. On further analysis, we found that this misjudgement is likely due to the ESP agent never experiencing a loss due to such a bottom lane attack during training.

## 7 SUMMARY

We introduced the ESP model for producing meaningful and sound contrastive explanations for RL agents. The key idea is to structure the agent’s action-value function in terms of meaningful future predictions of its behavior. This allows for action-value differences to be compared in terms of deltas in the future behaviors they entail. To achieve meaningfulness, we required the agent designer to provide semantic features of the environment, upon which GVFs were learned. To achieve soundness, we ensured that our explanations were formally related to the agent’s preferences in a well-defined way. Our case studies provide evidence that ESP models can be learned in non-trivial environments and that the explanations give insights into the agent’s preferences. An interesting direction for future work is to continue to enhance the internal structure of the GVFs to allow for explanations at different levels of granularity, which may draw on ideas from GVF networks (Schlegel et al., 2018).

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-structured approach to enhancing explainability in reinforcement learning through the novel Embedded Self-Prediction (ESP) model. The core idea—embedding human-interpretable generalized value functions (GVFs) directly into the Q-function to enable contrastive, feature-based explanations—is conceptually strong and addresses a significant gap in explainable RL. The clarity of exposition is generally high: the motivation is convincingly laid out, especially in the defense of manually-designed features, and the technical foundations are grounded in established RL theory, such as GVFs and Bellman operators. The integration of integrated gradients and minimal sufficient explanations further strengthens the practical utility of the framework, particularly for high-dimensional feature spaces. However, a key concern lies in the potential fragility of the circular dependency between the policy and the self-predicting GVFs, which, while acknowledged, may pose challenges for stability and generalization beyond the presented case studies. Although the ESP-DQN algorithm provides an empirical mitigation, the theoretical convergence analysis is limited to the tabular setting, leaving open questions about scalability and robustness in more complex environments with deep function approximation. Additionally, while the use of hand-crafted features is well-justified, the framework's dependence on them could limit broader applicability in domains where such semantic features are not readily available or are costly to engineer. Overall, the work makes several valuable contributions—architectural innovation, explanation methodology, and empirical validation across diverse tasks—but would benefit from deeper discussion of its assumptions and limitations, particularly regarding feature design and training dynamics.

comments.title: Mismatch in Discount Factor Notation Between MDP and GVF Definitions
comments.quote: A policy _π_ maps states to actions and has Q-function _Q[π]_ ( _s, a_ ) giving the expected infinite-horizon _β_ -discounted reward of following _π_ after taking action _a_ in _s_ .
comments.explanation: The Q-function is defined using discount factor _β_, but later in the GVF definition, the discount factor is denoted _γ_. While it's common in RL literature to use _γ_ for discounting, and the paper does consistently use _γ_ for GVFs, the initial use of _β_ for the standard Q-function introduces an inconsistency. The paper never clarifies whether _β_ and _γ_ are intended to be the same or different. In context, since the ESP model embeds GVF predictions into Q-value estimation, it would be expected that both discounts align—otherwise, the combining function _C_[ˆ] would need to reconcile different time horizons. The convergence analysis in Section 3 treats _γ_ and _β_ separately: _γ_ for GVF accumulation and _β_ for reward discounting. This is mathematically valid, but the lack of explicit distinction early on creates ambiguity. A careful reader might initially assume they are the same, leading to confusion about whether the value and GVF predictions operate on the same timescale. Given that the model's soundness hinges on the alignment of internal predictions with actual Q-values, this notational divergence should be clarified early.

comments.explanation: The text describes a feedforward process—map to GVF, then apply _C_[ˆ]—but this contradicts the self-predicting nature of the model. The GVF _Q_[ˆ] _F_ depends on the greedy policy _π_[ˆ], which in turn depends on _Q_[ˆ] via _C_[ˆ] applied to _Q_[ˆ] _F_, creating a circular dependency. The passage implies a unidirectional flow, but the model is inherently recursive. Although the paper acknowledges this circularity in Section 2, the initial presentation in the figure caption and surrounding text misleads readers into thinking the computation is sequential, potentially raising confusion about infinite recursion during training. Later clarification using target networks resolves the issue in practice, but the early exposition lacks sufficient caveats for rigorous understanding.

The lack of constructive methods for ensuring Bellman sufficiency undermines the practical relevance of the theoretical result. comments.title: Ambiguity and Inconsistency in GVF Feature Design and Description for CartPole
comments.quote: For ESP-continuous, we have 12 features, the first 8 pre-terminal GVF features corresponding to the delta features of the cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity for left and right sides. The 4 terminal GVF features are indicators of whether the episode ended by moving out-of-bounds to the left or right or the pole fell below the termination agle to the left or right.
comments.explanation: Multiple inconsistencies arise in the CartPole GVF description. First, 'delta features for left and right sides' is ambiguous—continuous state variables are one-dimensional; 'sides' may imply signed deltas or binned indicators, but the paper does not specify encoding. Second, the description excludes a GVF feature for episodes ending by time limit (successful completion), which violates the stated design of capturing all terminal conditions. Third, the claim that discretized features in ESP-discrete also serve as terminal features contradicts the schema's semantic separation between pre-terminal and terminal roles. Lastly, calling one variant 'continuous' falsely suggests real-valued GVF outputs, when in fact both variants use indicator features. Together, these issues create confusion about feature encoding, design principles, and reproducibility.

comments.title: Inaccurate Interpretation of Delta GVF Features
comments.quote: The GVF value for these features can be interpreted as the future change in the variables’ values.
comments.explanation: Describing the GVF of a delta feature as representing 'the future change' is misleading. The GVF accumulates discounted step-wise changes: E[∑_t γ^t Δx_t], which is a weighted average of future changes, not the total net change (x_final − x_0). Only under γ=1 and finite horizon does it telescope to total change. With γ < 1, it reflects a smoothed trend. The terminology suggests a direct prediction of future difference, which is incorrect and could mislead in theory or feature design. A more accurate phrasing would be 'cumulative discounted change' or 'expected accumulation of future step-wise differences'.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Minimal Sufficient Explanations (MSX) Definition and Selection
comments.quote: Also, for an arbitrary subset of indices _E_ , let _S_ ( _E_ ) =[] _i∈E[|]_[∆] _[F,i]_[(] _[s, a, b]_[)] _[ ·][ θ][i]_[(] _[s, a, b]_[)] _[|]_[ be the total magnitude] of the components, which lets the preference be expressed as _S_ ( _P_ ) _> S_ ( _N_ ).

comments.title: Unjustified Claim About DQN-full Failure Due to Feature Bottleneck
comments.quote: Interestingly, DQN-full with 17 features consistently fails to learn, which we hypothesize is due to the extreme 17 feature bottleneck inserted into the architecture.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes DQN-full's failure to a '17-feature bottleneck', but this claim is poorly supported. While DQN-full shares the ESP-DQN architecture, it lacks GVF supervision. The real cause of failure may be the absence of auxiliary GVF losses that provide inductive bias and regularization, not the bottleneck itself. The fact that DQN-full succeeds with 131 features suggests increased capacity compensates for missing supervision, not that the bottleneck is the primary issue. The explanation oversimplifies and misattributes causality. A more accurate interpretation would contrast architectural constraints with the benefits of multi-task learning via GVF prediction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in ESP Model Diagram Description: Causal Loop Misstatement
comments.quote: The model first maps a state-action pair ( _s, a_ ) to a GVF vector _Q_[ˆ] _[π] F_[ˆ][of][the][agent’s][greedy][policy] ˆ _π_ ( _s_ ) = _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ). This vector is then processed by the combining function _C_[ˆ] , which produces a Q-value estimate _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ).
comments.explanation: The text describes a feedforward process—map to GVF, then apply _C_[ˆ]—but this contradicts the self-predicting nature of the model. The GVF _Q_[ˆ] _F_ is defined with respect to the greedy policy _π_[ˆ], which depends on _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) = _C_[ˆ]( _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ ))—a circular dependency. The passage implies a one-way flow, but the model is inherently circular: _Q_[ˆ] _F_ depends on _π_[ˆ], which depends on _Q_[ˆ], which is output of _C_[ˆ] applied to _Q_[ˆ] _F_. The intended meaning is clear from later sections—this is acknowledged in Section 2 as 'circularly defined'—but the initial description in the figure caption and surrounding prose can mislead a reader into thinking the computation is sequential. This could lead to confusion about how training is possible without infinite recursion. While the authors resolve this later, the early presentation lacks the necessary caveats for a rigorous reader.

comments.title: Potential OCR Artifact in Bellman GVF Operator: Misplaced 'ex-'
comments.quote: the corresponding _n_ -dimensional GVF, denoted _Q[π] F_[(] _[s, a]_[)][,][is][the][ex-] pected infinite-horizon _γ_ -discounted accumulation of _F_
comments.explanation: The word 'expected' is split as 'ex- pected', which is likely due to OCR or page-breaking artifact rather than author error. Context fully resolves this: 'expected' is used correctly in adjacent sentences, and the mathematical definition matches the standard expectation of accumulated features. This is not a conceptual issue and would not mislead a reader. However, it is a textual artifact that slightly degrades readability. Since it's clearly not a semantic error and is resolved by context, it does not rise to the level of even minor severity.

comments.title: Inadequate Justification for Bellman Sufficiency Definition
comments.quote: **Definition 1** (Bellman Sufficiency) **.** _A feature and hash function pair_ ( _F, h_ ) _is_ Bellman sufficient _if_ ˆ _for any ESP model Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) = _C_[ˆ] ( _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ )) _with greedy policy π and state-action pairs_ ( _s, a_ ) _and_ ( _x, y_ ) _, if h_ ( _Q_[ˆ][+] _F_[(] _[s, a]_[)) =] _[ h]_[( ˆ] _[Q]_[+] _F_[(] _[x, y]_[))] _[ then][ B]_[[ ˆ] _[Q]_[](] _[s, a]_[) =] _[ B]_[[ ˆ] _[Q]_[](] _[x, y]_[)] _[, where][Q]_[ˆ][+] _F_[=] _[ B] F[π]_[ˆ][[ ˆ] _[Q][F]_[ ]] _[.]_
comments.explanation: The definition of Bellman Sufficiency is critical for Theorem 1, but its motivation and interpretation are underspecified. The condition says that if two GVF vectors hash to the same value, then their Bellman backups must be equal. But why is this sufficient for convergence? The reader is not shown how this enables stable learning or prevents divergence. This contrasts with standard Q-learning convergence conditions (e.g., finite state space, appropriate step sizes), and the paper provides no comparison or intuition. While the proof is in Appendix B, the lack of explanation leaves the definition looking abstract and unmotivated. Moreover, the notation _[ ˆ] _[Q]_[+] _F_ is unclear—this seems to be _Q_[ˆ] _F_ after one Bellman backup, but the subscript notation is nonstandard. Given that this condition underpins the theoretical claim, more justification or an example would be warranted. This is a real gap: a reader cannot assess the practical plausibility of the condition without further context.

comments.title: Missing Specification of Hash Function in ESP-Table
comments.quote: for _C_[ˆ] we assume a hash function _h_ that maps its continuous GVF inputs to a finite table. For example, since GVFs are bounded, this can be done with arbitrarily small error via quantization.
comments.explanation: While quantization is mentioned, the exact form of _h_ is left unspecified—e.g., uniform binning, learned discretization, etc.—and no parameter settings (e.g., number of bins) are given. Crucially, the convergence result (Theorem 1) depends on the assumption that _h_ is 'locally consistent', but no guidance is provided on how to design or verify such a function in practice. This makes the result theoretically valid but hard to reproduce. In real implementations (especially with neural networks), this abstraction does not translate directly—hence, the lack of specification undermines the bridge from theory to practice. While acceptable in a conference paper with space limits, this omission qualifies as underspecification for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of self-predicting GVF in ESP model
comments.quote: the ESP model embeds a GVF _Q[π] F_[ˆ][of][the][agent’s][greedy][policy] _[π]_[ˆ][into] the agents Q-function _Q_[ˆ] , via _Q_[ˆ] ( _s, a_ ) = _C_[ˆ] ( _Q_[ˆ] _F_ ( _s, a_ ))
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the circularity implied by the phrase 'GVF of the agent’s greedy policy π̂' while simultaneously using that GVF to define the Q-function from which π̂ is derived. This creates a potential definitional loop: Q̂_F depends on π̂, but π̂ = argmax Q̂, and Q̂ depends on Q̂_F. The authors do acknowledge this in the subsequent section as a 'circular dependence', but the initial presentation in Section 2 does not make explicit how this circularity is resolved during training — specifically, whether the GVF is trained to predict under the current greedy policy or if there's a target policy used in Bellman backups. However, context resolves this: later in Section 3.2 (GVF Update), they clarify that the GVF is trained using the target network's greedy action, so the dependence is broken via target networks. While not ideal for a first-time reader, the method is sufficient for empirical implementation. Therefore, this is not a true error but a pedagogical ambiguity in presentation.

comments.title: Incomplete statement of Bellman sufficiency definition
comments.quote: Second, we assume the pair _C_ ˆ table after Bellman GVF backups (see Section 2) with respect to representing Bellman backups. ( _F, h_ ) is _Bellman Sufficient_ , which characterizes the representational capacity of the
comments.explanation: The text breaks off mid-sentence just before Definition 1. This creates a misleading impression that the paragraph is defining both the assumptions and their consequences in a single sentence. The fragment 'assume the pair C̄ table after Bellman GVF backups...' appears to be garbled, likely due to OCR or transcription error. The plausible intended meaning, based on the subsequent Definition 1, is that they assume (F, h) is Bellman sufficient — but the actual assertion is lost in this sentence. However, Definition 1 itself is clearly stated and self-contained. A careful reader can infer that the broken sentence was meant to introduce or motivate the definition. Since the core definition follows and makes sense independently, and given that this likely results from OCR corruption rather than author error, no deeper issue remains. Thus, we treat this as a presentation artifact.

comments.title: Misalignment in GVF update target between prose and formal operator definition
comments.quote: we update _θF_ based on L2 loss at the output of _Q_[ˆ] _F_ with respect to a target value _yi_ = _Fi_ + _γQ_[ˆ] _[′] F_[(] _[s] i[′][,]_[ ˆ] _[a][′] i_[)]
comments.explanation: Initially, this appeared consistent with the Bellman GVF operator defined earlier: BF^π[QF](s,a) = F(s,a) + γ ∑_{s'} T(s,a,s') QF(s',π(s')). However, in the training update, the target uses Q̂'_F(s'_i, â'_i) where â'_i = argmax_a Q̂'(s'_i, a), i.e., the greedy action under the target Q-function. But this corresponds to the policy π̂' derived from the target network — not necessarily the policy π̂ implied by the current GVF Q̂_F. The Bellman GVF operator requires the policy π to be consistent with the QF being predicted. Here, the policy used in the update (via â'_i) is derived from the full Q-function Q̂' = C̄'(Q̂'_F), not directly from Q̂'_F. Hence, unless C̄' is invertible or Q̂'_F directly represents the same information, π̂' may differ from what π would be if derived only from Q̂'_F. This breaks the semantic consistency of the GVF prediction: it’s learning Q-values under a policy that is not self-predicted by the GVF itself, but by the combined Q-function. This undermines the 'self-predicting' claim unless additional assumptions hold. The issue persists even after contextual checks — the method is underspecified regarding whether the policy used in the GVF backup is intended to be π̂_F (greedy w.r.t. some interpretability-based policy from GVF) or π̂ (greedy w.r.t. Q̂). The training algorithm clearly uses the latter, but this weakens the interpretability argument: the GVF is not self-predicting the policy it governs but one derived externally.

comments.title: Overstated generality in explanation soundness claim for non-linear C̄
comments.quote: Thus, IGX(s, a, b) = ⟨∆F(s, a, b), θ(s, a, b)⟩ is a sound explanation
comments.explanation: The authors claim that Integrated Gradients (IG) yields a 'sound' explanation for any differentiable C̄, meaning that the dot product of attributions and feature differences exactly recovers the Q-value difference. While IG does guarantee this property under linearity of the path integral (Sundararajan et al., 2017), its accuracy depends on the interpolation path and the function’s non-linearity. More critically, the property _C̄(Xsb) + θ(s,a,b)·(Xsa − Xsb) = C̄(Xsa)_ (i.e., error-free attribution) only holds exactly in expectation under certain conditions, and finite approximations (like finite-difference integration) introduce error. The paper does not quantify or bound this error, nor does it discuss cases where the gradient may be ill-behaved (e.g., saturation in deep networks). While IG is widely used, calling it 'sound' in the same sense as linear decomposition raises the bar — 'sound' implies correctness, not just heuristic usefulness. Given that practitioners may interpret this as a guaranteed decomposition, the claim is stronger than what can be rigorously established without additional assumptions. But since this is standard terminology in the explainable AI literature and the result (Eq. 1) is mathematically correct under ideal IG computation, this is more a matter of framing.

comments.title: Underspecification of hash function h in ESP-Table convergence analysis
comments.quote: for Ĉ we assume a hash function h that maps its continuous GVF inputs to a finite table
comments.explanation: The convergence analysis for ESP-Table relies on a quantized representation via a hash function h with 'local consistency'. However, no constructive definition or practical method for ensuring Bellman sufficiency is provided. The hash function must preserve Bellman-equivalent values: if two GVF vectors produce the same Bellman backup, they must hash to the same value. But without knowledge of the true dynamics or value structure, designing such an h is non-trivial. The claim that 'this can be done with arbitrarily small error via quantization' assumes that sufficiently fine quantization preserves Bellman sufficiency, which is questionable — coarse quantization could merge states with different Bellman backups, violating the condition. While locally consistent h is necessary, it's not sufficient for Bellman sufficiency. The analysis hinges on this assumption, but it's unclear how to satisfy it in practice beyond trivial cases. This weakens the theoretical contribution, though it remains a reasonable asymptotic idealization.

comments.title: Incorrect Integrated Gradient Formula and Misattributed Key Property
comments.quote: The key property is that the IG weights linearly attributes feature differences to the overall output difference, i.e. _C_ ˆ( _Xsb_ ) = _θ_ ( _s, a, b_ ) _·_ ( _Xsa − Xsb_ ).
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the equation _C_ˆ(Xsb) = θ(s, a, b) · (Xsa − Xsb) was dimensionally and logically incorrect — a scalar function evaluated at Xsb cannot equal a dot product involving a difference vector. Checking the surrounding context, I saw that the authors are likely attempting to express the integrated gradient (IG) property that the total difference _C_ˆ(Xsa) − _C_ˆ(Xsb) equals θ(s, a, b) · (Xsa − Xsb), which is the well-known completeness property of IG. The correct form appears just after in Equation (1), where they write _Q_ˆ(s,a) − Q_ˆ(s,b) = θ(s,a,b) · ∆F(s,a,b). However, the incorrect version before Equation (1) is not merely a typo — it misstates a foundational property of IG, suggesting that the function value at the baseline equals the attribution sum. This is not true, and such a claim undermines the theoretical grounding of their explanation method. Even under OCR error assumptions, the left-hand side _C_ˆ(Xsb) cannot be reconciled with the intended meaning. The correct statement should be _C_ˆ(Xsa) − _C_ˆ(Xsb) = θ(s,a,b) · (Xsa − Xsb), which is exactly what Equation (1) correctly asserts. But because the prose before Equation (1) presents a false property as the 'key property', it risks misleading the reader about how IG works.

comments.title: Ambiguous and Incorrect Definition of S(E) in Minimal Sufficient Explanations
comments.quote: Also, for an arbitrary subset of indices _E_ , let _S_ ( _E_ ) =[] _i∈E[|]_[∆] _[F,i]_[(] _[s, a, b]_[)] _[ ·][ θ][i]_[(] _[s, a, b]_[)] _[|]_[ be the total magnitude] of the components, which lets the preference be expressed as _S_ ( _P_ ) _> S_ ( _N_ ).
comments.explanation: At first, I assumed the symbol [] was an OCR artifact for summation (∑), which is reasonable given the context. After that correction, the definition S(E) = ∑_{i∈E} |∆F,i(s,a,b) · θi(s,a,b)| makes sense as a sum of absolute contributions. However, the following claim — 'which lets the preference be expressed as S(P) > S(N)' — is problematic. The actual preference magnitude is ∑_i θi ∆F,i = ∑_{i∈P} θi ∆F,i + ∑_{i∈N} θi ∆F,i = S(P) − S(N), since for i∈N, θi ∆F,i < 0, so |θi ∆F,i| = −θi ∆F,i. Therefore, the sign of the preference depends on whether S(P) > S(N), but the magnitude is S(P) − S(N). So while the inequality S(P) > S(N) is correct as a condition for a > b being preferred, it is misleading to say the preference is 'expressed as' this inequality, as if the difference in sums were equivalent to the preference value. More importantly, in the MSX optimization, they define the problem as finding the smallest E ⊆ P such that S(E) > S(N). But S(E) sums absolute values, while the actual cumulative positive contribution from E is ∑_{i∈E} θi ∆F,i, which is just the unsigned sum since these terms are positive. Using absolute values here is redundant and potentially confusing, but not logically incorrect if we assume it's meant to emphasize magnitude. However, the larger issue is that S(N) is defined as a sum of absolute values, so S(N) = −∑_{i∈N} θi ∆F,i. Then the condition S(E) > S(N) becomes ∑_{i∈E} |θi ∆F,i| > −∑_{i∈N} θi ∆F,i, which is exactly ∑_{i∈E} θi ∆F,i > −∑_{i∈N} θi ∆F,i = ∑_{i∈N} |θi ∆F,i|, which ensures that the total positive contribution from E exceeds the total negative contribution — so the condition is correct. However, the notation is unnecessarily confusing: using absolute values in the definition while the underlying contributions are already signed in the definition of P and N obscures the algebraic simplicity. A cleaner formulation would define V(E) = ∑_{i∈E} θi ∆F,i and require V(E) > −∑_{i∈N} θi ∆F,i. As written, a careful reader may question whether the absolute value is distorting the true contribution. The ambiguity does not break correctness but reduces clarity and could mislead.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Symbol in IG Attribution Formula
comments.explanation: The integral expression appears corrupted by OCR: '1 ... 0' likely intends to represent ∫₀¹. Similarly, '_∂Xsa,i_' should probably be ∂/∂X_i or ∂/∂α_i, but the use of _Xsa,i_ is inconsistent. In standard IG, the integral is of the partial derivative of _Ĉ_ with respect to the i-th input component, evaluated along the linear path from Xsb to Xsa. The correct form is θi(s,a,b) = ∫₀¹ [∂Ĉ(Xsb + α(Xsa−Xsb)) / ∂Xi] dα. The notation '_∂Xsa,i_' is misleading because Xsa is a fixed vector, not a variable of differentiation. The path is parametrized by α, and we're differentiating with respect to the i-th component of the input to _Ĉ_. While '_∂Xsa,i_' might be a shorthand for 'the i-th component of the input vector', it conflicts with standard notation where the variable of differentiation is local, not a fixed endpoint. Given the context and the standard IG formulation, this is likely an OCR-induced inconsistency rather than a conceptual error. Still, as written, it could confuse a reader unfamiliar with IG into thinking the derivative is with respect to the endpoint value rather than the running input. Since the paper's audience includes ML researchers who may not be experts in attribution methods, precise notation matters. However, because the surrounding prose correctly describes interpolation and the gradient, and Equation (1) is correct, the issue is resolvable from context, making it a notation clarity issue rather than a fundamental flaw.

comments.title: Ambiguity in interpretation of delta GVF features
comments.quote: We also consider delta GVF features that are equal to the change in a variable across a time step. The GVF value for these features can be interpreted as the future change in the variables’ values.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the description of delta GVF features suggests that they are defined as the immediate change in a variable (e.g., Δx_t = x_{t+1} − x_t), and their corresponding GVF Q^π_F(s,a) would then represent the expected accumulated sum of such changes over time. However, the phrase 'the future change in the variables’ values' is ambiguous: it could be misread as predicting just the final change (e.g., x_∞ − x_0) rather than the sum of step-wise deltas. This is a concern because cumulative sum of deltas is not equivalent to total net change unless the process is deterministic and converges—otherwise, transient fluctuations contribute. In stochastic environments like Lunar Lander or CartPole, the GVF for a delta feature will reflect expected cumulative deviation, not net terminal change. However, the paper’s schema is internally consistent: delta features are used within the standard GVF framework with γ-discounted sums, so Q^π_F,i(s,a) = 𝔼[∑_t γ^t f_i(s_t,a_t)] where f_i(s_t,a_t) = Δx_t. Thus, the GVF tracks cumulative adjusted change, not endpoint difference. The context resolves the core issue—the GVF formalism ensures correct interpretation—but the phrasing in the prose risks misleading a reader into thinking these features predict net future change rather than accumulated step-wise differences. A more precise phrasing would be 'cumulative discounted change' or 'expected accumulation of step-wise changes'.

comments.title: Incomplete specification of terminal GVF features in CartPole
comments.quote: For ESP-continuous, we have 12 features, the first 8 pre-terminal GVF features corresponding to the delta features of the cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity for left and right sides. The 4 terminal GVF features are indicators of whether the episode ended by moving out-of-bounds to the left or right or the pole fell below the termination agle to the left or right.
comments.explanation: The text states there are 4 terminal GVF features indicating termination causes, but the description only lists four mutually exclusive events: out-of-bounds left/right and pole fall left/right. This appears complete at first. However, in CartPole, episodes can also end after 500 steps without failure—this is a third type of termination. The reward function gives +1 per step, so reaching 500 steps yields maximum reward. The omission of a GVF feature for 'successful completion by time limit' is a concern, because terminal features are supposed to encode all possible end conditions. If no feature activates in successful episodes, then the GVF vector will be zero for terminal components, possibly misrepresenting the agent’s success. The GVF loss (Figure 2) is low, suggesting good fit, but this could be achieved through pre-terminal features alone. Still, the terminal GVF definition schema in Section 6.1 says terminal features 'describe basic conditions at the end of the episode'—which should include normal completion. The absence of this feature is not addressed, and while not mathematically invalid (the model could still learn), it breaks the stated design principle. This leads to underspecification: the method assumes complete coverage of terminal modes, but one is omitted without justification. It's possible the 'safely landing' indicator from Lunar Lander is repurposed, but in CartPole there is no such natural analog.

comments.title: Ambiguity in MSX subset selection method
comments.quote: We select a solution that has the largest positive weight by sorting P and including indices into the MSX from largest to smallest until the total is larger than S(N).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'largest positive weight' is ambiguous: does it refer to the largest θ_i(s,a,b), ∆F,i(s,a,b), or the product ∆F,i · θ_i (i.e., the contribution magnitude)? The MSX objective depends on S(E) = Σ |∆F,i · θ_i| for i ∈ E, so the sorting should be by the magnitude of contribution. The passage says “largest positive weight” — but 'weight' could be misread as θ_i alone. However, in the definition of S(E), it's the absolute value of the product that defines the magnitude. Moreover, P is defined as {i : ∆F,i · θ_i > 0}, so within P, the sign is positive and thus S(E) = Σ ∆F,i · θ_i for E ⊆ P. This means that sorting by the contribution value (∆F,i · θ_i) is correct. The context supports this interpretation: earlier, the bar visualizations are labeled with θ_i · ∆F,i. The authors likely mean 'largest contribution', but because they use the term 'weight', which was earlier used for θ_i (e.g., 'attribution weight vector'), a careful reader might initially think sorting is done by θ_i alone. This is a subtle but potentially misleading ambiguity. It’s resolvable with full context, but in isolation, it presents a risk of incorrect implementation.

comments.title: Inconsistency between CartPole GVF description and earlier schema
comments.quote: For ESP-discrete, there are 8 pre-terminal GVF features that discretize the state variables into meaningful regions... These discretized features also double as the terminal features, since they capture the relevant aspects of the state at termination...
comments.explanation: According to the schema introduced in Section 6.1, terminal and pre-terminal features are distinguished: terminal features describe final state conditions (e.g., out-of-bounds), while pre-terminal ones derive from variables active during the episode. The passage claims the same 8 discretized features serve as both pre-terminal and terminal, which contradicts this distinction. This raises a concern: are terminal features a subset of pre-terminal ones or separate? In the ESP-discrete version, if a feature indicates 'cart out-of-bounds', it may fire intermittently during the episode (pre-terminal) and remain active at termination (terminal). But the GVF is defined as the expected discounted accumulation—so the terminal aspect isn't cleanly separated. The issue is not mathematical: the GVF still learns a value. But it violates the intended semantic separation that supports interpretable explanations. For example, a user might expect terminal features to reflect only the final state, not transient excursions. The authors get the modeling right—GVFs are trained appropriately—but the conflation of roles undermines the clarity of the schema. This could mislead designers applying the method to other domains where clean separation matters (e.g., safe vs. unsafe final states).

comments.title: Ambiguity in GVF feature count for ESP-continuous in CartPole
comments.quote: For ESP-continuous, we have 12 features, the first 8 pre-terminal GVF features corresponding to the delta features of the cart position, cart velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity for left and right sides.
comments.explanation: The passage states there are 8 pre-terminal delta features for 4 state variables (position, velocity, angle, angular velocity), with 'for left and right sides' possibly implying two per variable. But each state variable is continuous and one-dimensional — position is scalar, not directional. The phrase 'for left and right sides' suggests polarity-based splitting (e.g., separate features for positive and negative delta). But then it says '8 pre-terminal GVF features', which matches 2 per variable × 4 variables = 8. Then adds 4 terminal features, totaling 12. That part checks out. However, the description 'delta features... for left and right sides' is misleading: it's not that there are separate deltas for left vs right motion, but rather that positive and negative changes are naturally captured by signed delta values. The GVF accumulation can then reflect net trend. If features were encoded as two separate indicators (e.g., 'delta_position > 0', 'delta_position < 0'), that would be different. But the paper doesn't clarify whether delta features are raw (signed) values or binned indicators. Given that earlier the authors distinguish between continuous delta features and discretized ones, it’s likely these are signed continuous features. Still, the phrasing 'for left and right sides' risks misinterpretation as directional indicators rather than signed scalars. This could affect reproducibility: a reader might implement two binary features per delta instead of one continuous one. While not a mathematical error, it's underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous Interpretation of 'Continuous GVF Features' in CartPole
comments.quote: For the control problems, CartPole (with discrete and continous GVFs features) and LunarLander, we see that all agents are statistically indistinguishable near the end of learning and reach peak performance after about the same amount of experience. We see that the discrete feature version of CartPole converged slightly faster than the continous version, but the difference is relatively small.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the contrast between 'discrete' and 'continuous' GVF features in CartPole appears to suggest a fundamental difference in how continuous variables are represented—perhaps one uses delta features (continuous-valued outputs) while the other uses indicators. However, based on the PAPER SUMMARY, this interpretation needs refinement. The 'ESP-discrete' variant for CartPole uses 8 indicator features (2 per variable) for safe regions of cart position, velocity, pole angle, and angle velocity. The 'ESP-continuous' variant, despite the name, does not use delta GVF features for the pole or cart variables (as delta features are only explicitly mentioned for Lunar Lander and Tug of War), but rather uses terminal GVF features as indicators of failure mode (left/right boundary or pole angle violation). Thus, both variants use indicator features, but for different abstractions: pre-terminal safe regions vs. terminal failure conditions. The term 'continuous' here likely refers to the underlying state variables being continuous, not to the use of delta-based or real-valued GVF features. This creates ambiguity: a reader might assume 'continuous GVF features' implies delta encodings or real-valued outputs, but no such features are defined for CartPole in the summary. While the passage isn't mathematically incorrect, the naming risks misleading readers about the nature of the comparison. The authors correctly distinguish the variants, but the labeling could conflate representation type with variable type.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Pre-terminal GVF Features' in Lunar Lander Description
comments.quote: The pre-terminal GVF features (Lunar Lander): distance-to-goal, velocity, tilt-angle, right/left landing leg in goal position, main/side engine use.
comments.explanation: This list mixes variable types and representation styles. Distance-to-goal, velocity, and tilt-angle are continuous state variables; if used directly as GVF features with γ > 0, their GVFs would represent expected accumulated change (if delta features) or expected time in certain regions (if discretized). However, the description treats them all as pre-terminal GVF features without specifying whether they are encoded as delta or indicator features. Later, in the explanation section, velocity is used as a feature in IGX, suggesting it contributes meaningfully to the explanation. But if it's used as a raw continuous feature with γ > 0, its GVF would represent long-term accumulation of velocity—not a meaningful quantity. The more plausible interpretation, consistent with the schema in Section 6.1, is that these are discretized (indicator) features (e.g., 'high downward velocity'). The PAPER SUMMARY lists them without specifying encoding, yet the Lunar Lander GVFs are used analogously to CartPole's discrete features in explanations. This creates ambiguity: are these delta or indicator features? The schema section allows both, but the intended encoding must be clear for reproducibility. Given that 'delta GVF features' are separately defined and applied in Tug of War (for damage), and that interpretable explanations arise from meaningful accumulations, it’s likely these are intended as indicator features. Still, the omission of encoding specification in the Lunar Lander feature list is a gap.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim about DQN-full Failure Due to '17-Feature Bottleneck'
comments.quote: Interestingly, DQN-full with 17 features consistently fails to learn, which we hypothesize is due to the extreme 17 feature bottleneck inserted into the architecture. This is supported by seeing that with 131 features DQN-full does learn, though more slowly than ESP-DQN.
comments.explanation: The claim attributes DQN-full’s failure specifically to the '17-feature bottleneck,' implying that the limited feature space hampers learning. But DQN-full shares ESP-DQN’s architecture and only differs in that it lacks explicit GVF training—its final layer combines raw input (after feature extraction) directly into Q-values, bypassing the GVF bottleneck. Wait: according to the PAPER SUMMARY, 'DQN-full shares ESP-DQN’s network architecture but lacks explicit GVF training, while Vanilla DQN removes the GVF-combiner bottleneck by connecting directly to raw input.' This contradicts the passage's explanation. The passage implies DQN-full has the bottleneck, but the summary says it’s Vanilla DQN that removes it—so DQN-full should still have the bottleneck. But then why does DQN-full with 131 features succeed while 17 fails? The authors’ hypothesis makes architectural sense only if the bottleneck is the issue. However, the real issue may not be the bottleneck itself but the lack of inductive bias from GVF supervision. ESP-DQN benefits from auxiliary GVF losses that regularize and guide learning; DQN-full, despite the same architecture, lacks this and may struggle with credit assignment in sparse-reward settings like ToW. The failure with 17 features might stem not from bottleneck size but from insufficient signal to train meaningful representations without GVF guidance. The support—'with 131 features DQN-full does learn'—might reflect greater representational capacity compensating for lack of supervision. But the causal attribution to 'bottleneck' is weak. The explanation oversimplifies and misattributes the failure mechanism. While the observed behavior is plausible, the justification is not well-supported by the architectural definitions.

comments.title: Misleading Terminology: 'Delta GVF Features' Interpreted as Change Rather Than Accumulation
comments.quote: b) We also consider delta GVF features that are equal to the change in a variable across a time step. The GVF value for these features can be interpreted as the future change in the variables’ values.
comments.explanation: The term 'future change' is potentially misleading. A GVF accumulates the discounted sum of feature values over time. If the feature is the delta (change) of a variable per time step, then the GVF represents the discounted sum of future changes, not the total future change. For example, if Δxₜ = xₜ₊₁ − xₜ, then Q(π)F(s,a) = E[∑ γ^k Δxₜ₊ₖ | π], which equals E[∑ γ^k (xₜ₊ₖ₊₁ − xₜ₊ₖ)] = E[(1−γ)xₜ₊₁ + γ(1−γ)xₜ₊₂ + ⋯ + γ^k(1−γ)xₜ₊ₖ₊₁ + ⋯] + boundary terms—a weighted average of future values, not the total change. Only if γ = 1 and the episode is finite does this sum telescope to E[x_final − xₜ], the total future change. But γ < 1 in RL settings for stability. Thus, the GVF does not represent 'future change' but a discounted accumulation of step-wise deltas, which correlates with but is not equivalent to total change. The authors’ intent is clear: use delta features to make GVFs sensitive to directional trends. And in practice, such features can capture meaningful dynamics. The interpretation is approximately valid for γ close to 1. But strictly speaking, the claim overreaches. A careful reader might conclude that the GVF directly predicts Δx_future, which is incorrect. This is a subtle but important distinction in temporal credit assignment. The issue is not fatal, but could mislead in theoretical analysis or feature engineering.

comments.title: Underspecification of Monte Carlo Estimation for GVF Accuracy
comments.quote: Specifically, given the ESP policy π ˆ at any point, we can use Monte-Carlo simulation to estimate Q[π] F[ˆ][(s, a)] for all actions at a test set of states generated by running π ˆ.
comments.explanation: The method for evaluating GVF accuracy is conceptually sound: use rollouts under the current policy to estimate the expected discounted accumulation of features. However, critical implementation details are missing. How many rollouts per state-action pair? How is the discount factor γ handled in the Monte Carlo estimate—does it match the GVF training γ? How are terminal states treated? Is the estimate averaged over multiple trajectories or a single one? The test set is said to be 'generated by running π ˆ', but then GVF estimates are needed for all actions, including non-greedy ones. So counterfactual actions must be taken in the test states, requiring on-policy exploration or importance sampling. The passage does not specify whether actions are taken according to π ˆ or if all actions are evaluated from each test state. Without this, a reader cannot reproduce the evaluation. The authors correctly aim to validate GVF fidelity, and Monte Carlo is the right approach. But the lack of specification—number of samples, horizon, action selection in test states—makes the method underspecified. This is particularly important because GVF accuracy is a core claim supporting explainability.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated framework for addressing the "black-box" nature of reinforcement learning (RL) agents by grounding action-value functions in human-understandable features. The proposed Embedded Self-Prediction (ESP) model is conceptually innovative, as it elegantly bridges the gap between opaque deep Q-networks and interpretable Generalized Value Functions (GVFs) by enforcing a direct, self-consistent constraint between the two. The writing is clear, the technical contributions—ranging from theoretical convergence proofs to the application of integrated gradients for minimal sufficient explanations—are well-structured, and the inclusion of a complex real-time strategy game provides a rigorous testbed for the methodology. The primary challenge identified, which is typical of such neuro-symbolic or feature-aligned approaches, lies in the potential brittleness of relying on hand-engineered features; however, the authors provide a reasonable defense for this trade-off. Overall, the paper demonstrates high technical maturity and offers a promising, sound approach to advancing interpretability in deep RL.

comments.title: Mathematical and Notational Inconsistency in Integrated Gradient Formulation
comments.quote: C^(Xsb) = θ(s, a, b) · (Xsa - Xsb) ... θi(s, a, b) = 1 ∂C^(Xsb + α· (Xsa−Xsb)) ∂Xsa,i d α
comments.explanation: The paper contains multiple technical errors regarding the Integrated Gradient (IG) attribution property. The provided equation for linear attribution is dimensionally and logically inverted, and the definition of the attribution weights θ is malformed (including OCR/formatting errors, missing integral symbols, and ambiguous derivative indexing). While Equation 1 elsewhere in the text appears correct, these errors significantly undermine the formal definition of the proposed MSX algorithm.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Greedy Policy Variable Definition
comments.quote: The model first maps a state-action pair ( s, a ) to a GVF vector _Q_[^] _[π] F_[^][of][the][agent’s][greedy][policy] ^ _π_ ( _s_ ) = _Q_[^] ( _s, a_ ).
comments.explanation: The caption and surrounding text contain garbled symbols and appear to mathematically conflate the greedy policy (π(s) = argmax Q(s, a)) with the Q-value itself. This creates confusion regarding the policy derivation mechanism described in Section 2.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Self-predicting' GVF Definition
comments.quote: The embedded GVF is self-predicting in the sense that it is predicting values of the greedy policy for which it is being used to compute.
comments.explanation: The phrasing is logically ambiguous, creating confusion as to whether the GVF computes the policy or the policy computes the GVF. This exacerbates the potential misunderstanding of circular dependencies in the agent's architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Delta' GVF feature interpretation
comments.quote: The GVF value for these features can be interpreted as the future change in the variables’ values.
comments.explanation: The text conflates a discounted accumulation of future changes with the absolute difference ($x_{final} - x_{current}$). Given $\gamma < 1$, the GVF does not strictly represent the future change, which may lead to incorrect assumptions regarding what the agent is actually tracking.
